pi_items.source: Task definition and electrical physics verification: 240V is used as the heating element's rated voltage. This is a standard North American voltage.
Task definition and electrical physics verification: 5000W is used as the heating element's rated power output.

Web search confirmed 208V is a real, standard three-phase voltage in North American commercial/industrial systems (derived from 120/208V wye distribution). See: https://quick220.com/pages/north-american-voltage-ranges
Electrical physics calculation verified: R = V²/P = 240²/5000 = 57600/5000 = 11.52Ω exactly. Calculation confirmed by Python execution.
Electrical physics calculation verified: P = V²/R = 208²/11.52 = 43264/11.52 = 3755.5556W, which rounds to 3755.56W. Calculation confirmed by Python execution.
Verifier.py line 62 hardcodes tolerance as 10.0 W, not 5 W. The acceptable range is [3745.56, 3765.56] W, a ±10W band around the expected 3755.56W value.
Verified in export_result.sh (line 6) and setup_task.sh (line 8): the output file path is correctly specified.

privileged_info_summary: This is a synthetic electrical engineering calculation task. The heating element has a rated power of 5000W at 240V, yielding a fixed resistance of 11.52Ω. When operated at 208V (a standard North American industrial voltage), the derated power is 3755.56W. The task requires the agent to use the HSN Electrical Calculations app (package com.hsn.electricalcalculations, available on Google Play Store) to perform these Ohm's Law calculations and save the result to /sdcard/Download/derated_power.txt. One discrepancy exists: the metadata claims a tolerance of 5W, but the verifier uses 10W tolerance.
